title: Tenant Identity Record and FQDN Semantic Contract
description: Draft semantic contract for tenant identity records, FQDN ownership, and tenant lookup semantics required by the authentication first slice. The first implementation slice requires tenant ownership to fail closed for unresolved hosts, inactive custom domains, and cross-tenant domain ownership conflicts.
aiGuidance: Use this as a source-backed tenant identity contract. Do not infer tenant routing or FQDN ownership from source paths without updating semantic instances.

lifecycle.description: Tenant identity covers provisioning of a tenant record and resolution of request hosts to that tenant: apex, reserved, invalid, and malformed hosts resolve to no tenant, seeded slug FQDNs resolve with a slug host source, and active verified custom domains resolve through the domain registry with a custom-domain host source. Provisioning is operator-driven and host resolution is a deterministic per-request derivation.

stateModel.description: State includes the organisation id, slug/FQDN, a derived realm name of the form tenant-{organisationId}, a host source (slug, custom_domain, or fixture), and the per-FQDN domain-registry states for custom domains. A custom domain only contributes tenant context once it is verified and active.

permissions.description: Tenant provisioning is operator-controlled and gated by platform tenant read/create permissions, while host-identity resolution is read-only request context that grants no data access without the route's own permission checks. Tenant isolation is preserved because resolution never produces a partial or fallback tenant.

contracts.description: The operation surface provides tenant provisioning and a host-identity lookup, with runtime resolution consuming the request Host / forwarded-host headers. The contract returns the resolved tenant context or an explicit no-tenant result.

validation.description: Host validation classifies apex, slug, local slug, reserved, unknown, malformed, host-with-port, and possible-custom-domain cases, and forwarded-host precedence is enforced deterministically. Custom domains must be verified and active to be accepted.

errorModel.description: Invalid, unknown, reserved, apex, and inactive/pending/disabled custom hosts resolve fail-closed to a null tenant context rather than to a partial or fallback tenant, and provider failure returns a typed degraded result without fabricating tenant state. No untrusted host is ever silently served as a tenant.

auditModel.description: Tenant provisioning is audited at the provisioning surface, while host-identity resolution is read-only and carries no mutation audit, with failed resolution logged rather than audited. Audit precedes the provisioning change.

readinessModel.description: Readiness is invariant-ready once the resolver and domain registry are available, and degraded mode disables registry-dependent mutation while keeping read-only resolution and audit visible. Live proofs skip honestly when seeded local substrate is absent rather than faking tenant resolution.

proof.description: A runtime domain-identity matrix proof validates host classification plus live slug resolution, and a custom-domain resolution proof validates live registry resolution with rejection of inactive, pending, and disabled domains and a unique-enabled-domain guard. Proof is hermetic/runtime lineage; fresh USF proof pending USF-101/USF-59.

uiSemanticDefinition.description: Tenant identity resolution is a request-context contract for this slice, not a user-facing UI journey.
